content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 102v–103v | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_005_page_001.jpg, DEU_005_page_002.jpg; source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/DEU_005_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_005_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Moses summoned all Israel and said to them:

"Hear, O Israel, the statutes and the ordinances I am declaring in your hearing today. Learn them and be careful to follow them. **2** The LORD our God made a covenant with us at Horeb. **3** It was not with our fathers that the LORD made this covenant, but with us — all of us who are alive here today. **4** Face to face the LORD spoke with you at the mountain, from the midst of the fire — **5** though I was standing between the LORD and you at that time to tell you what the LORD said, because you were afraid of the fire and would not go up the mountain. He said:

**6** "'I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the place of slavery.

**7** "'You shall have no other gods before me.

**8** "'You shall not make for yourself an idol — any carved image of anything in the heaven above, or on the earth below, or in the waters below the earth. **9** You shall not bow down to them or serve them. For I, the LORD your God, am a jealous God, visiting the guilt of the parents on the children — to the third and fourth generation of those who reject me, **10** but showing covenant-faithfulness to the thousandth generation of those who love me and keep my commandments.

**11** "'You shall not misuse the name of the LORD your God, for the LORD will not hold anyone guiltless who misuses his name.

**12** "'Keep the Sabbath day holy, as the LORD your God commanded you. **13** Six days you shall labor and do all your work, **14** but the seventh day is a Sabbath belonging to the LORD your God. On it you shall not do any work — you, or your son or your daughter, or your male or female servant, or your ox or your donkey or any of your livestock, or the sojourner who lives within your gates — so that your male and female servants may rest just as you do. **15** Remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt and the LORD your God brought you out from there with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm. That is why the LORD your God commanded you to keep the Sabbath day.

**16** "'Honor your father and your mother, as the LORD your God has commanded you, so that you will live long and so that things will go well with you in the land the LORD your God is giving you.

**17** "'You shall not murder.

**18** "'You shall not commit adultery.

**19** "'You shall not steal.

**20** "'You shall not give false testimony against your neighbor.

**21** "'You shall not covet your neighbor's wife. You shall not crave your neighbor's house, or his field, or his male or female servant, or his ox or his donkey, or anything else that belongs to your neighbor.'

**22** "These are the words the LORD spoke to your whole assembly at the mountain — out of the fire, the cloud, and the thick darkness — with a great voice. He added nothing more. He wrote them on two stone tablets and gave them to me.

**23** "When you heard the voice from the darkness, while the mountain was blazing with fire, all your tribal leaders and elders came to me **24** and said: 'The LORD our God has shown us his glory and his greatness, and we have heard his voice from the fire. Today we have seen that God can speak with a person and the person still lives! **25** But now — why should we die? This great fire will consume us. If we hear the voice of the LORD our God any longer, we will die. **26** For who among all flesh has ever heard the voice of the living God speaking from the midst of fire — as we have — and survived? **27** You go near and hear everything the LORD our God says, and then tell us everything he tells you. We will listen and obey.'

**28** "The LORD heard what you said when you spoke to me, and the LORD said to me: 'I have heard what this people said to you. Everything they said is good. **29** If only their hearts would be inclined to fear me and keep all my commands always, so that it would go well with them and their children forever!

**30** "'Go, tell them to return to their tents. **31** But as for you — stand here with me, and I will give you all the commandments, statutes, and ordinances you are to teach them, so they may follow them in the land I am giving them to possess.'

**32** "So be careful to do what the LORD your God has commanded you — do not turn aside to the right or to the left. **33** Walk in all the ways the LORD your God has commanded you, so that you may live and it will go well with you, and you will live long in the land you are going to possess."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Dynamic choices — PNEUMA:**

**V.3 — "not with our fathers...but with us — all of us who are alive here today":** This is one of the most theologically charged verses in Deuteronomy. Moses collapses the distance between the Horeb generation and the present one. Every generation of Israel stands at Sinai. PNEUMA's emphasis on "all of us" and "here today" drives this home with force.

**V.4 — "Face to face":** Hebrew panim el panim — the same phrase used of Moses' intimacy with God in Exodus. Yet in v.5 Moses immediately qualifies: he was intermediary. PNEUMA preserves both — the direct encounter and the mediation — without trying to resolve the tension.

**V.9 — "visiting the guilt of the parents on the children":** Hebrew poked avon avot al banim — "visiting iniquity of fathers upon sons." This corporate, generational view of guilt is uncomfortable to modern ears. PNEUMA does not soften it. The severity is part of the covenant's gravity; it is matched by thousandfold grace in v.10.

**V.10 — "covenant-faithfulness":** Hebrew hesed — the project's established rendering for this covenant-relational term. Here it runs to "the thousandth generation" — the contrast with the "third and fourth" of v.9 is intentional and staggering. Grace vastly outscales judgment.

**V.12–15 — The Sabbath commandment in Deuteronomy vs. Exodus:** Deuteronomy's version grounds the Sabbath in the Exodus (v.15: "you were a slave in Egypt") rather than in creation (Exod 20:11). PNEUMA preserves this significant difference — it is part of the book's distinctive theology. The Sabbath here is about liberation, not cosmic order.

**V.21 — "You shall not covet...You shall not crave":** The Hebrew uses two different verbs: lo tachmod (covet) for the wife, and lo tit'avveh (crave/desire) for the house and other things. Deuteronomy splits what Exodus presents as a single prohibition. PNEUMA uses two distinct English verbs to honor this textual distinction.

**V.22 — "He added nothing more":** Hebrew velo yasaf — "and he did not add more." This is a statement about the completeness and sufficiency of the Ten Words. PNEUMA renders it as a simple declarative sentence that lands with finality.

**V.29 — "If only their hearts would be inclined to fear me":** The lament in God's voice is striking and devastating — God yearns for what he cannot compel. The "if only" (mi yitten) is a Hebrew idiom of wistful longing. PNEUMA preserves it as an optative expression of divine desire.

**Vaticanus (LXX) observation:** Vaticanus pages 147–148 cover this chapter. The LXX Decalogue in Deuteronomy preserves the same major differences from the Exodus version as the MT. Notably at v.15 the LXX reads "dia touto eneteilato soi kyrios ho theos sou" — "therefore the LORD your God commanded you" — identical structure to MT. At v.21, the LXX order differs from MT: the LXX places "field" before "wife" in the coveting command, an interesting textual variant visible in the Vaticanus. Vaticanus page 147 clearly shows the CAP.VI marker indicating chapter division between chapters 5 and 6 of Deuteronomy in the Vaticanus chapter system (which differs from modern numbering in some books).
